name: iSkin: Flexible, Stretchable and Visually Customizable On-Body Touch Sensors
short_description: A thin, flexible, stretchable on-skin sensor overlay that turns the body's surface into a touch input area for mobile and wearable computing.
long_description: iSkin is a class of skin-worn touch sensors built from biocompatible silicone with embedded conductive carbon-black electrodes, combining capacitive and resistive sensing to detect touch at two pressure levels even when the overlay is stretched by 30% or bent to a 0.5 cm radius. Produced in customizable shapes for the finger, forearm, or ear, it supports buttons, sliders, and click wheels worn directly on the skin, enabling discreet, always-available input. Presented at ACM CHI 2015 (Best Paper Award), it matters to physical AI and interaction design as an early on-body input surface that explores how computation can become continuous with the human body rather than confined to a rigid device.
ai_note: iSkin is a passive capacitive/resistive touch-sensing material; touch detection is signal thresholding with no AI or machine learning involved.

images.caption: iSkin stretchable on-body touch sensor overlay worn on the skin as a customizable input surface.
images.alt_text: A thin, semi-transparent silicone sensor sticker with printed touch buttons applied directly to a person's skin, used as a touch input surface.

sources.title: iSkin: Flexible, Stretchable and Visually Customizable On-Body Touch Sensors for Mobile Computing
iSkin - Martin Weigel Research Project Page

interactions.title: Decline a call by pinching your finger
interactions.description: With a sensor strip worn along a finger, you press it to silence or decline an incoming call, or press harder using the second pressure level to answer — no reaching for the phone in your pocket.

interactions.title: Music ClickWheel printed on your skin
interactions.description: A round, LP-styled sticker acts as a four-zone rotational click wheel: you swipe and tap around it to skip tracks and adjust the volume of a music player, the way you would on an old iPod, but worn on the body.

interactions.title: EarSticker for a headset
interactions.description: A thin sensor conforms to the back of the ear so you can tap or press near the earpiece to control a headset — accepting a call or pausing audio with a discreet touch instead of fumbling for an inline button.

interactions.title: A keyboard you wear on your forearm
interactions.description: A keypad-shaped sticker applied to the forearm turns a stretch of skin into a typing surface, letting you tap out and send short messages without a screen.

interactions.title: Two-level press: tap versus push
interactions.description: Because the overlay senses two pressure levels, a light tap and a firm push register as different commands — so one widget can both, say, lower the volume on a light touch and skip a track on a hard press.

interactions.title: Design your own patch in a graphics program
interactions.description: Before fabrication you draw the sensor's shape and look in an ordinary graphics editor — one example mimics musical notation, another is a circular LP — so the touch surface doubles as personal body decoration that still reads buttons and sliders.

interactions.title: Eyes-free control for a head-mounted display
interactions.description: A forearm slider acts as the input surface for smart glasses or an AR headset, letting you scrub through a menu or notification by feeling for the strip on your arm rather than waving in mid-air — an extension of iSkin's role as an input surface for other devices.

interactions.title: Discreet quick-replies during a meeting
interactions.description: A small finger-worn patch maps firm and light presses to a couple of canned text responses, so you can fire off an "on my way" without pulling out a phone or visibly looking down.
